slides.title: Gift-Wrap Any Function
slides.body: A **decorator** adds behavior to a function — logging, timing, validation — without changing a single line of the original. It's a reusable wrapper you snap on with one line.
slides.tip: Think of it as gift wrap: the present inside never changes, you just add a new layer around it.

slides.title: Functions Are Values Too
slides.body: In Python, **functions are first-class objects** — you can store them in variables and pass them around just like numbers or strings. No call, no run.
slides.code:
def say_hello():
    print("Hello!")

greeter = say_hello   # no () = no call
greeter()
slides.tip: greeter and say_hello point to the exact same function object.

slides.title: Build Your First Decorator
slides.body: A decorator is a function that **takes a function, wraps it, and returns the wrapper**. The wrapper runs extra code before and after the original.

slides.title: @ Is Just Syntactic Sugar
slides.body: Writing `@my_decorator` above a `def` is **exactly the same** as writing `func = my_decorator(func)` right after it — just cleaner to read.
slides.code:
# These two are identical:
@shout
def greet(): ...

# same as:
def greet(): ...
greet = shout(greet)
slides.tip: The @ symbol is pure shorthand — Python rewrites it for you before anything runs.

slides.title: Works on Any Function — Use *args
slides.body: Make your wrapper accept `*args, **kwargs` and forward them so your decorator works on **any function**, regardless of its parameters.
slides.tip: Always return the result — forgetting it makes your function silently return None.

slides.title: You Get Decorators Now!
slides.body: You can write a decorator that wraps **any** function with reusable behavior. You'll spot `@staticmethod`, `@property`, `@app.route`, and `@functools.lru_cache` everywhere — all the same pattern you just learned.
slides.tip: Always add @functools.wraps(func) inside your wrapper to keep the original function's name and docstring.
